# Thread: My lips were blue!!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chrystin.comeau@live.com — 2012-03-26T18:06:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6404>

Is that bad? It certainly was a little off-putting, that's for sure! I've been doing CT for a month or so. coldest water was just below 50 degrees. That was to my belly button. The past two days the water has been slightly warmer (low 50's) but I've submerged to my neck. This morning when i got out after about twenty minutes, I looked in the mirror and my lips were blue!

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-26T19:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6437>

> **cjco;6440 wrote:** Is that bad? It certainly was a little off-putting, that's for sure! I've been doing CT for a month or so. coldest water was just below 50 degrees. That was to my belly button. The past two days the water has been slightly warmer (low 50's) but I've submerged to my neck. This morning when i got out after about twenty minutes, I looked in the mirror and my lips were blue!

I've never experienced this. I would say take it slower. Perhaps others with more experience can jump in.

### JC2K — 2012-03-26T19:58:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6455>

Blue lips = moderate hypothermia. You'll want to take it slower. Less time if you're submerging the whole body.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-03-26T20:01:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6456>

i've gotten blue lips from swimming in 80F water before. Not sure what the significance is.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-26T20:05:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6459>

I got blue lips when I was doing my scuba certification. The instructor wouldn't let anyone else go into the ocean (under 40 degree water) until my lips got some color in them. We waited over two hours. I was not the most popular gal that day!!! Funny thing is that I haven't seen them since that day about 20 years ago. My sis used to get them all the time going in any water.

Now sometimes doing CT some of my fingernails turn a little blue. Weird thing is that I don't even put my hands in the water!!!

### chrystin.comeau@live.com — 2012-03-26T20:15:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6464>

> **JC2K;6491 wrote:** Blue lips = moderate hypothermia. You'll want to take it slower. Less time if you're submerging the whole body.

Thanks. like I said, I've been doing this for a month or so. Colder water, even. But previously I had only submerged up to just under my breasts. This was my second time going up to my neck. I'm back to shivering, too, which Ia haven't had in a while. Also, my skin is much less red than it used to get on my legs. Now only my abdomen gets red. Even with the full submersion, I don't think my top half got red .....

### Lyndra — 2012-03-26T23:24:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6586>

Back in my windsurfing/waterskiing days, I often got blue lips when I got really cold (lakes in Colorado don't get very warm in the summer). I'd have to sit in the car with the heater running full blast for 20 minutes to warm up. I never got blue lips skiing, even on frigid days, so I think it's the water immersion that so effectively reduces core body temperature. During CT, I would avoid staying in so long that your lips turn blue.

### taryn.ourso@gmail.com — 2012-03-27T01:56:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6674>

cjco,

I've experienced this a few times myself. I asked Dr. Kruse about this awhile back, and he said it happens to him sometimes, too, and that it is not harmful if you are otherwise well. Check your core temperature, though...if you have blue lips AND are dropping your core temp, then back off for sure. Personally, it doesn't take much for me to get blue lips...I check my temp, and if all is well, then I know I'm good. Also, be sure you are checking in on yourself regularly...check your skin color, feeling, etc...you may just be prone to having blue lips.

### chrystin.comeau@live.com — 2012-03-27T02:01:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6680>

> **lsutiger143;6710 wrote:** cjco,
>
>
>
> I've experienced this a few times myself. I asked Dr. Kruse about this awhile back, and he said it happens to him sometimes, too, and that it is not harmful if you are otherwise well. Check your core temperature, though...if you have blue lips AND are dropping your core temp, then back off for sure. Personally, it doesn't take much for me to get blue lips...I check my temp, and if all is well, then I know I'm good. Also, be sure you are checking in on yourself regularly...check your skin color, feeling, etc...you may just be prone to having blue lips.

Thanks. I felt fine, other than being cold and shivering (which stopped but started again once I've started full submersion). Is an oral temp OK?

### taryn.ourso@gmail.com — 2012-03-27T02:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6688>

> **cjco;6716 wrote:** Thanks. I felt fine, other than being cold and shivering (which stopped but started again once I've started full submersion). Is an oral temp OK?

Yeah, oral temp is all I do. Believe me, I was freaked out the first time I walked in my bathroom from my pool and saw the blue lips/ghost face!!! But, I know from the past that even if I would eat ice cream or alot of ice, my lips would lose some color. If you are still worried, you can always have someone hang around while you CT. You feeling fine otherwise?

### taryn.ourso@gmail.com — 2012-03-27T02:15:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-lips-were-blue.447/post-6689>

Oh, wait, you said you did feel fine. I should learn to read =)

# Thread: Is Lyme a circadian disease, like fleas and parasites that go away in winter?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-03-26T20:34:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-6475>

I am seeing that pyroluria is now thought to be LYME. Lyme is thought to be epigenetic as well as tick borne. Shoot, dogs catch worms and people can too.

I found Dr Kruse because my daughter was misdiagnosed with mental illness (unlike fat people with an inflammatory brain disease, hey, I'm ocd and like the company LOL)and one of my dear friends had RA/Lyme disease. I had a rheumatoid virus once and we were just comparing notes. He mother also had severe epilepsy brought on by the accidental death of her brother. The first seizure was at about 12 years old and she was standing in front of a class nervous and had a petite ma and wound up standing in a puddle of pee. (maybe pyroluria) She also had hashi's, along with all of the big boobied, full- lipped women in her family.

It seems that stress is the thing and weather triggers epilepsy, active ms, parkinsons, pyroluria, etc.

When you think about it, pyroluria cures the damage of lyme. You isolate and stop eating. You have an infection=depression=inflammation, the not eating starts growth and repair, and what grows? The bones. Lyme destroys the bones.

Also, my kid flew to the southern hemisphere for winter/our summer. She started feeling better on the way there and worse on the way back, twice. She didn't eat on the plane and barely drank. Her joints were in so much pain she could barely walk. I couldn't imagine the trip would have bound her up so much.

So then, during the sunspots, my lyme disease friend stayed off facebook and could barely get out of bed. She started antibiotics. Another friend of hers was diagnosed with ms after a few iffy years during the sunspots. I am wondering if anyone with ms,lyme,parkinsons, als, shizophrenia, or garden variety depression have been diagnosed with pyroluria?

### chocolate — 2012-03-27T00:06:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-6611>

Also, LYME disease and fat are not complementary. Pyrolurics tend to be muscular, even as women. If it was an epigenetic virus, maybe adaptations occurred during gestation.

[http://www.nutritional-healing.com.au/content/articles-content.php?heading=Pyroluria](http://http://www.nutritional-healing.com.au/content/articles-content.php?heading=Pyroluria)

### chocolate — 2012-03-27T03:15:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-6722>

[http://www.lymediseaseresource.com/wordpress/magnesium-friend-or-foe-to-chronic-lyme-disease/](http://http://www.lymediseaseresource.com/wordpress/magnesium-friend-or-foe-to-chronic-lyme-disease/)

lyme disease need magnesium for its biofilms, summer fruit and sugar release magnesium and mercury adheres in its place. Is mercury overload from lyme stealing the magnesium?

I know lyme has active times and I am sure summer must be it. Cold thermogenesis is the only way to get rid of mercury and repatriate the magnesium I am thinking. And then, defrost with anti biotics in hand? or at least defrost ready to test. The presoak has to be sugar free.... I am guessing good old cider vinegar for the malic acid.

### Destiny — 2012-03-27T03:22:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-6726>

> **chocolate;6758 wrote:** [http://www.lymediseaseresource.com/wordpress/magnesium-friend-or-foe-to-chronic-lyme-disease/](http://http://www.lymediseaseresource.com/wordpress/magnesium-friend-or-foe-to-chronic-lyme-disease/)
>
>
>
> lyme disease need magnesium for its biofilms, summer fruit and sugar release magnesium and mercury adheres in its place. Is mercury overload from lyme stealing the magnesium?

Great link! Thx!

### chocolate — 2012-04-24T18:14:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-18382>

[http://www.anapsid.org/cnd/diffdx/irondisease.htm](http://http://www.anapsid.org/cnd/diffdx/irondisease.htm) This gal has iron overload and lyme with a nice one page article.

lt seems the iron does go with the neurodegenerative diseases. Anecdotally.

### chocolate — 2012-05-01T03:06:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-20252>

[http://jada.ada.org/content/137/12/1643.1.full](http://http://jada.ada.org/content/137/12/1643.1.full)

MOLECULE THAT INDUCES BIOFILM DISPERSION DISCOVERED

A biologist at Binghamton University, Binghamton, N.Y., reportedly has discovered a molecule that induces the dispersion of biofilms.

David Davies, PhD, an associate professor of biology, says he has found and is in the process of synthesizing a compound that will cause biofilm colonies to disperse, leaving bacteria up to 1,000 times more susceptible to disinfectants, antibiotics and immune functions.

Biofilms are complex aggregations of bacteria marked by the excretion of a protective and adhesive matrix. They develop almost anywhere that water and solids or solids and gases meet, which means they are virtually everywhere. When traveling alone in planktonic form, most bacteria are of small consequence and generally easy to manage. However, when they form biofilms, bacteria seem to gain super powers.

The small molecule Dr. Davies is working with appears to be one of the few known examples in nature of a communication signal that remains effective across species, family and phyla.

### chocolate — 2012-05-01T03:13:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-20257>

[http://jb.asm.org/content/191/5/1393.abstract](http://http://jb.asm.org/content/191/5/1393.abstract)

A Fatty Acid Messenger Is Responsible for Inducing Dispersion in Microbial Biofilms▿

David G. Davies\* and

ClÃ¡udia N. H. Marques

\+ Author Affiliations

Department of Biological Sciences, State University of New York at Binghamton, Binghamton, New York 13902

ABSTRACT

It is well established that in nature, bacteria are found primarily as residents of surface-associated communities called biofilms. These structures form in a sequential process initiated by attachment of cells to a surface, followed by the formation of matrix-enmeshed microcolonies, and culminating in dispersion of the bacteria from the mature biofilm. **In the present study, we have demonstrated that, during growth, Pseudomonas aeruginosa produces an organic compound we have identified as cis-2-decenoic acid, which is capable of inducing the dispersion of established biofilms and of inhibiting biofilm development.** When added exogenously to P. aeruginosa PAO1 biofilms at a native concentration of 2.5 nM, cis-2-decenoic acid was shown to induce the dispersion of biofilm microcolonies. This molecule was also shown to induce dispersion of biofilms, formed by Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Proteus mirabilis, Streptococcus pyogenes, Bacillus subtilis, Staphylococcus aureus, and the yeast Candida albicans. Active at nanomolar concentrations, cis-2-decenoic acid appears to be functionally and structurally related to the class of short-chain fatty acid signaling molecules such as diffusible signal factor, which act as cell-to-cell communication molecules in bacteria and fungi.

### AmyJ — 2012-05-02T02:15:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-20562>

I watched an interesting documentary on a few people with lyme disease on Netflix last weekend. "Under Our Skin". Pretty eye-opening. Counting my blessings, I don't have it so bad at all.

### chocolate — 2012-05-20T17:29:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-25693>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2009/02/090209205151.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2009/02/090209205151.htm)

To establish infection, however, the bacterium also must acquire a number of essential nutrients, including metals like **manganese** from its mammalian and tick hosts. Until now, no metal transporter responsible for this acquisition had been identified in this bacterium.

Lead author Dr. Zhiming Ouyang, postdoctoral researcher in microbiology at UT Southwestern, said another newly discovered characteristic about the bacterium that causes Lyme disease is that it doesn't seem to require iron to function, something most other pathogens need to survive.

lithium and manganese are cofactors ....interesting

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-20T18:11:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-25712>

Because the spirochete uses the lymph and fat system as part of it life cycle CT is part of the solution but you still must clear the toxin........liberation of it is not enough for many. And yes I do think Lyme has a serious circadian component.

### chocolate — 2012-05-21T00:10:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-lyme-a-circadian-disease-like-fleas-and-parasites-that-go-away-in-winter.448/post-25835>

[http://www.samento.com.ec/sciencelib/4lyme/beyondantibiotics.html](http://http://www.samento.com.ec/sciencelib/4lyme/beyondantibiotics.html)

My buddy forwarded me this really well thought out list of things to try for lyme. I guess there is a lifecycle disruptor from Japan that looks like a tens machine. It sends off frequencies that vary and don't make for happy little spirochetes. Supposedly they lose their groove. the scheduling and frequency has to change a lot.

This is another protocol...

The KMT program #4 is designed for treatment of mycoplasma (6).

H. The spirochetes and their close relatives ( Bartonella, Rickettsia, Ehrlichiosis, Brucella abortis) are best treated last - with antimicrobial herbs or antibiotics., 1 tsp bid. We use an alternating course of teasel root tincture (15 drops 3 times per day) for 6 weeks and then TOS free cat’s claw tincture (10 drops tid). We also use Echinacea root tincture , 2 dropperfull 3 times/day. Organic freeze dried garlic sometimes has a profound effect on the spirochetes. Many other herbs have enormous potential in the treatment of chronic Lyme disease.

**Frequency #1 in the KMT TENS unit inhibits the microbial growth of spirochetes and Bartonella, simultaneously activates specific immune responses and aids the uptake of antimicrobial herbs.

Injected bee venom has long been my favorite during this phase of the treatment (22, 23). The peptide mellitin has strong antibiotic activity against all spirochetes (24). Bee venom also contains nerve growth factor, the very substance needed for healing, when everything else has been attended to.

For the psychiatric presentations of Lyme disease I use large doses of Niacin. Niacinamide and no-flush Niacin do not work. 3-6 gms in 3-4 divided doses often show amazing results. It appears that Niacin has tremendous antibiotic potential against all types of Borrelia (12). I suspect that our mentor and genius in orthomolecular psychiatry, Abraham Hoffer, MD discovered a treatment for

**

Bb long before Lyme-disease was known.

The current antibiotic protocols are discussed and listed elsewhere (10). My favorites include Zithromax and Minocycline (both work symbiotically by binding to separate regions of the bacterial 50s ribosomal nucleic acid and both inhibit the microbes from taking part in protein transcription). I also use Rifampin.

[http://customers.hbci.com/~wenonah/new/9steps.htm](http://http://customers.hbci.com/~wenonah/new/9steps.htm)

The Klinghardt Neurotoxin Elimination Protocol

Approved by:

American Academy of Neural Therapy and

Institute of Neurobiology (Bellevue, WA, USA)

Institute for Neurobiologie (Stuttgart, Germany)

Academy for Balanced NeuroBiology Ltd (London, United Kingdom)

This lecture was presented by Dietrich Klinghardt M.D., Ph.D. at the Jean Piaget Department at the University of Geneva, Switzerland Oct. 2002 to physicians and dentists from Europe, Israel, several Arab countries and Asia.

> The Basic Program
>
>
>
> High protein, mineral, fatty acid and fluid intake
>
>
>
> Rationale:
>
>
>
> Proteins provide the important precursors to the endogenous metal detox and shuttle agents, such as coeruloplasmin, metallothioneine, glutathione and others. **The branched-chain amino acids in cow and goat whey have valuable independent detox effects**.
>
> Metals attach themselves only in places that are programmed for attachment of metal ions. Mineral deficiency provides the opportunity for toxic metals to attach themselves to vacant binding sites. A healthy mineral base is a prerequisite for all metal detox attempts (selenium, zinc, manganese, germanium, molybdenum etc.). Substituting minerals can detoxify the body by itself. Just as important are electrolytes (sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium), which help to transport toxic waste across the extra-cellular space towards the lymphatic and venous vessels.
>
> Lipids (made from fatty acids) make up 60â€“80% of the central nervous system and need to be constantly replenished. Deficiency makes the nervous system vulnerable to the fat soluble metals, such as metallic mercury constantly escaping as odorless and invisible vapor evaporating from the amalgam fillings.
>
> Without enough fluid intake the kidneys may become contaminated with metals. The basal membranes swell up and the kidneys can no longer efficiently filtrate toxins. Adding a balanced electrolyte solution in small amounts to water helps to restore intra-and extra-cellular fluid balance

He seems to think once the mercury is gone, the other metals fall nicely into line detoxing. You need to have the proper minerals on board to do it.

# Thread: Hack my lack of desire for cold tub?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-26T22:28:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-6558>

Day 3 of baths.

Day one, two ten min baths.

Day two, two twenty min baths

Today - do not want.

After last nights, i was cold feeling through half the night. Am still feeling chilly now. I walked for an hour this am in 50 degree weather.

I have no instinct on what to do. I have the feeling i used to get before a hard planned exercise. Dread.

I know i can power through it and do it. But should i give body some recovery time? Just take a step back?

### hazyjane — 2012-03-26T23:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-6579>

Go slow with it! There were times I sensed that my body didn't want to do a cold bath, so I just iced instead or skipped CT that day. When I didn't listen to my body, I always had a hard time warming up. I figured out that if I hadn't slept well, it was not a good time to CT.

If I was feeling chilled already, I'd do an alternating hot/cold shower ending on cold, but I'd keep the cold parts short (this always warmed me up).

After a few weeks, there were days I started to crave the cold water!

### Sherry — 2012-03-26T23:13:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-6582>

I've been there with the lack of desire for jumping into the tub. Honestly, I just had to talk myself into it with the promises of all the benefits down the road because it sure isn't instant gratification for me. And if that doesn't work for me I don't do it. I've been trying to do 4-5 baths per week and if I don't have the desire some days I tell myself that the icing at night just has to suffice.

And, that lack of desire can very well be that the body needs some space. So, take that step back Cavemam and give yourself a hug for doing this in the first place. Think about how many people think we're nuts and have 1001 excuses of why not to do it. At least you're doing it no matter the times when you take a break or how hard it is to think about being in that cold water. We all deserve a hug for being here.

### Marsha — 2012-03-27T00:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-6627>

Go back to your face dunks...that relaxed you, right?

The other night I used the snorkel for awhile and tried to breathe very slowly - was quite nice! Then I decided I had to try the 'mammilian dive reflex' and held my breath for several dunks. Each time I went back in the water seemed warmer- but was still about 50 degrees.

I just read on the FACTOR X thread that cold water on the face/head makes everything else in the tub feel warmer. Don't know if I can make myself do it, but at least the seed is planted!

Last night got an ice burn while watching Frozen Planet, but will post about that at a more appropriate place.

Good luck, Cavemam - I've been feeling cold today, too, so you're not the only one!

### bonita — 2012-03-27T01:03:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-6646>

i agree with the others, go slow. i'm not really set up to do much with the ice packs, but i'm flush with cold water so i started with what i thought were cold showers (thermometer eventually told me otherwise). eventually i just sat down during a "cold" shower one day and put the plug in the tub. i started at 80F and originally started lying down--but that's actually much harder. shivered constantly, so i sat up and started lowering the temperature every time. (i also put towels under my butt and feet so they're not directly on the metal and started wearing my bra--it doesn't keep me warmer, it's just feels... i dunno, less vulnerable somehow.) after 2 weeks i found myself sitting in 55 degree water for 15 minutes.

now i start around 48-49 degrees (so it will still be under 55 when i'm done) and sit for 35-40 minutes and lie back for another 15. if you can only do a couple minutes lying back then just do that. interestingly in the link jack provided,

[http://www.me-cvs.nl/index.php?pageid=3076](http://http://www.me-cvs.nl/index.php?pageid=3076)

the doc there explains his protocol for water therapy (20 years ago) and he's basically suggesting the same thing i've done.

sometimes i have to push myself a bit to get to the tub, but it's not what i'd call a struggle. and the funny things is, i only have about 10 seconds of discomfort in the whole time i'm in the tub. when i first turn the water on and when it reaches my hoohaw and then when i lie back... the takes a few birthing breathes i must say. i am a chilled when i get out so i just pile on clothes and take the dog for a walk and come back toasty.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-27T02:13:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-6687>

> **bonita;6682 wrote:** i agree with the others, go slow. i'm not really set up to do much with the ice packs, but i'm flush with cold water so i started with what i thought were cold showers (thermometer eventually told me otherwise). eventually i just sat down during a "cold" shower one day and put the plug in the tub. i started at 80F and originally started lying down--but that's actually much harder. shivered constantly, so i sat up and started lowering the temperature every time. (i also put towels under my butt and feet so they're not directly on the metal and started wearing my bra--it doesn't keep me warmer, it's just feels... i dunno, less vulnerable somehow.) after 2 weeks i found myself sitting in 55 degree water for 15 minutes.
>
>
>
> now i start around 48-49 degrees (so it will still be under 55 when i'm done) and sit for 35-40 minutes and lie back for another 15. if you can only do a couple minutes lying back then just do that. interestingly in the link jack provided,
>
>
>
> [http://www.me-cvs.nl/index.php?pageid=3076](http://http://www.me-cvs.nl/index.php?pageid=3076)
>
>
>
> the doc there explains his protocol for water therapy (20 years ago) and he's basically suggesting the same thing i've done.
>
>
>
> sometimes i have to push myself a bit to get to the tub, but it's not what i'd call a struggle. and the funny things is, i only have about 10 seconds of discomfort in the whole time i'm in the tub. when i first turn the water on and when it reaches my hoohaw and then when i lie back... the takes a few birthing breathes i must say. i am a chilled when i get out so i just pile on clothes and take the dog for a walk and come back toasty.

Ok everyone, Thanks! I very much appreciate, and I think it's sage advice. I did end up sitting down in the cold tub naked this time, instead of donning the compression. Speaking of whoo-ha, YOOOUUUUCH. So I started shivering right away - and only spent 5 mins. I think I am going to back up to face dunks and icing until I want to try bath again. . .of course I'm shivery and cold right now. I bet once I start to feel warm again, I will be ready for the bath again. Hopeful. I also noticed the girls did NOT appreciate the water. Nip-objection at full blast.

### bonita — 2012-03-27T02:26:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-6694>

> **Cavemam;6723 wrote:** I also noticed the girls did NOT appreciate the water. Nip-objection at full blast.

no kidding. i wonder if that's where the expression "it's getting a bit nippy out" comes from? although interesting, only problematic for me on the right side, left is always fine, though not exactly relaxed.

just remember, any temperature below your body temperature creates a gradient that will lead to cold adaptation... just depends on how much time you wanna spend getting there. oh, and the girls should always be deferred to.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-27T19:15:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-7139>

I think you need to walk the line between pushing yourself and listening to your body. It's kind of like any sort of physical training - without some pressure to adapt, you won't, but if you overdo it, you risk going backwards either physically or mentally. Ask yourself what about the cold tub do you dread - is it the temperature, duration, or possibly both? Consider raising the temperature; Dr. K said any temperature differential would have a positive effect so it doesn't have to be right-out-of-the-tap cold. Or cut back the time - if 10 minutes sounds bearable, then do ten minutes. Wait until something inside you tells you to go for a lower temperature or longer duration and then go forward slowly. Maybe there's a boredom factor at work. Are you reading, listening to a podcast, or doing something that might help make the time go by faster? I'm all in favor of doing whatever works for you.

I find that the hardest point is right before getting in the tub, but once I do it, it's OK. I also give myself permission to get out early if that's what I feel I need that day. I also stay mindful of my motivation for doing this - the long-term improvements I am striving to make. That helps me get over the negative self-talk that tells me to skip it for today. I am also keeping a log of what I'm doing and how I feel. It's good to go back a few days and see how much better I'm feeling and how much easier it is to do.

### differentstory — 2012-03-28T18:25:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-7752>

I have a suggestion for you.

Don't fill the tub all the way up. I filled it all the way up in the beginning with 80 degree water thinking I would just keep stepping down the temp but I quickly started hating CT baths. So, instead, I did the coldest temperature I could and just did 3-4 inches of bath water and only did my butt and legs (at first crossed legged so my thighs didn't get in the water and then I started straightening them out). Once I felt COMPLETELY fine with that and could stay in and be warm for at least 30 minutes, I started pouring in more water and found that I transitioned from 4 inches to full emersion in a matter of 2 days! I was so stoked! The first time I had the water up to my chest and it was freezing cold (not me but the water per my husband), I started giggling uncontrollably. HA! I was thinking, "HOLY AMAZEBALLS! I am totally DOING THIS!!"

My husband gapes at me in awe.  Love it! It really should be only 30 seconds of pain and the rest is easy. So if it's not, do not put yourself through the misery.

My new thing is that I am having a hard time warming up. I find that it takes me 6+ hours to warm up (I'm not in very good shape). So, my new deal is that I only give myself an hour of natural warm-up and then I bring in electronic heat (stove, blanket, hot shower, whatever). Having what turns into an 8 hour CT session with how cold I feel afterwards was also making me want to quit, so now I'm giving myself a bit of a break. I absolutely know I'll get there. Maybe it will just take me 6 months.

I often picture myself jumping into a frozen pond while at Thanksgiving with the family and watching everyone gasp in shock and amazement. So fun!! That daydream alone keeps me going. HA!

Oh, also wanted to say, that I have found when you are cold-adapted that stepping the temp down 3-5 degrees is not a big deal in the least. So, I think the first few days I started with 60 degree water. Now, the water fluctuates anywhere from 55 to 60 and I really don't even notice. So don't feel like you need to do 50 right off the bat. Wait until you are cold-adapted. (With our water here I can't seem to get it down to 50 yet - I'm already at 1 milk jug of frozen water. I think I'm going to have to do 3. Thank goodness we have a big tub!)

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-28T19:16:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-7780>

Here's my approach: I get in the tub, _then_ turn the water on. It's much less bracing. The water just slowly migrates up over me. The only place I sort of "jump on in" is in the mountain stream I frequent on the weekends. That's much harder, but I don't stay in as long. I'm am, however, sort of getting addicted to a certain sensation: running down a mountain trail after a 20 minute soak in "snow run-off" cold water...pure euphoria. I might just start carrying a spear...

### agatha — 2012-03-28T23:13:46+0300

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-03-28T23:40:08+0300

### caroline — 2012-03-28T23:49:48+0300

### nonchalant — 2012-03-29T04:59:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-8096>

Cavemam, can you up the temperature? It took me weeks to get the temp down to 55F, and that was after a couple weeks of face-dunking at 50-55F. I look forward to my baths, and only the first one was shocking, at 65F. And I only up the time by 5 minutes, and that is only if I feel the last time was too easy.

### SimonM — 2012-04-01T01:26:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-9479>

> **Dan in Utah;7822 wrote:** Here's my approach: I get in the tub, _then_ turn the water on. It's much less bracing. The water just slowly migrates up over me.

Me too. I am a total whimp about being wet AND cold.

Isn't bitter melon extract supposed to make this easier?

### BJK77 — 2012-04-01T02:30:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-lack-of-desire-for-cold-tub.450/post-9577>

> **agatha;7981 wrote:** I don't do my bath if I'm feeling cold and tired - I feel like my body is telling me to slow down. Sometimes I have been cold and gone for a walk and felt warm enough to do my bath when I returned. I'm also not doing a bath tonight because I have a detox headache - I figure let the body clear this bunch of toxins before releasing more.

I'm the same. I have some days where I literally crave the cold bath - still don't enjoy it! - but do crave it and feel like I need it. Other days, like today when it's damp out and I spent 2 hours outside freezing this morning, my body has just had ENOUGH and I figure it needs a break to recover from what it has already endured. My toes are still trying to warm up from earlier and I've now been inside for 6 hours although we've been keeping the heater off so it's only 59 degrees in here!

I just think it's so important to listen to your body. I've been averaging 5 baths per week and I'm happy with that. The CT protocol suggests 2-5 times depending on your weight and health issues. I don't really have any weight to lose, just focusing on reversing my hypothyroidism and improving hormone levels. I'm hopeful that 5 days a week will get me where I need to be.

# Thread: opposite problem to Sandy....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opposite-problem-to-sandy.451/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### janagram — 2012-03-27T00:08:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opposite-problem-to-sandy.451/post-6615>

....known as constipation from VLC....what do you all do?

### Lyndra — 2012-03-27T00:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opposite-problem-to-sandy.451/post-6622>

Magnesium. I use magnesium citrate because it's cheap & easy to find. I take it first thing in the morning and then before bed, both times on an empty stomach to let it digest. It took quite a while for me to find the right dose and periodically I have to adjust it. I was a big user of "healthy whole grains" and needed a lot of magnesium when I transitioned to Paleo because of the fiber differences.

Dr. K has a lot of info about magnesium at [http://jackkruse.com/gnolls-com-opens-the-door-to-obesity-fight/](http://http://jackkruse.com/gnolls-com-opens-the-door-to-obesity-fight/)

### janagram — 2012-03-27T00:54:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opposite-problem-to-sandy.451/post-6644>

Lyndra...thanks. will get some mag citrate in case that works better than the malate I take.

### Guest — 2012-03-27T14:37:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opposite-problem-to-sandy.451/post-6923>

We will recover any problem so we will get a lot of satisfaction as well as perfect health and

remarkable goal so be conscious and happy for well growth.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-11T04:08:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opposite-problem-to-sandy.451/post-13586>

What say people about Potassium with the Magnesium. I know some ppl think you need K with Mg together...?

# Thread: From Central Washington
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sugarbiscuit — 2012-03-27T00:17:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/post-6623>

Hi All,

I first dieted in college, needing to "lose" weight when I went from 105 up to 131. Ha! I though I was fat then, but it was just beginning. Achieved this weight loss and then some (lowest 96 lbs) using a combo of starvation and recreational um, medications.

I eventually got back up to a healthier 125 and maintained for a while, gained up to about 135 slowly over the next few years. Then \*BAM\* I gained 40 pounds in about 3 months. That's when I got my PCOS diagnosis. The weight continued to creep up to top out at 198.

I've tried Body for Life, Atkins, low-fat, calorie restriciton. I hired a personal trainer for 8 months & lost a whopping total of 18 pounds during that time. Not to mention how tired I always was. I went on metformin and got pregnant & lost an additional 12 pounds in the first 4 weeks. (168 when I found out I was preggers).

For the last couple of years, I've been researching keto & paleo. I read Gary Tabues Good Calories, Bad Calories which led me to the Primal Blueprint and MDA. I also really enjoy reading Hyperlipid. That's how I ended up here.

The whole HCG/pregnancy connection really has my interest piqued. It was fairly obvious to me that whatever was going on with me during pregnancy was allowing me to shed weight, and fast. The whole paleo/keto thing makes sense, and I really like that Dr. Kruse breaks down his theories on a molecular level. I really need to know "why" about things and his posts fulfill that for me.

Now, to do the leptin reset and initiate CT. I **really** want to, but something, just something is holding me back. I wish I could figure out the psychological component. It so simple really....right? "Just do it." Why can't I?

### nonchalant — 2012-04-05T23:25:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/post-11912>

Hi Sugarbiscuit. Were you able to start CT at all?

You might try cool spongebaths, to ease into it.

### .lina — 2012-04-05T23:40:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/post-11921>

oooh you should just go for it, dear! especially the leptin reset was a great experience for me

### Melinda — 2012-04-06T01:13:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/post-11974>

Hi Sugarbiscuit, start with the Leptin Reset to get your basic circadian clock working. After 2-6 weeks, start slow with the face dunks. I think face CT feels wonderful and makes the skin look so pretty! I notice I have more desire to CT in a sunny situation like near my bathroom window in the afternoon when the sun pours in, not so much in the morning when it's in shade. You might want to consider a different "handle", Sugarbiscuit is the opposite of where you want to be going. Start now and keep a journal, I've had so many positive changes including weight loss but also better sleep and daytime energy.

### Sugarbiscuit — 2012-04-06T23:48:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/post-12280>

Haha, I really deliberated whether or not to use Sugarbiscuit, but its a name I've used for a long time, so I just stuck with it. To top it off, my real name is Kandy. It's not so much a food reference as a throwback to my Southern heritage (DH calls me "Sugar" and we both call my DD "biscuit").

Anyhow, I decided to try the LR again...started on Monday. It's going okay. I started freaking out a little last night with the cravings, but managed not to binge on anything somehow. I hit a rough patch at the grocery store today, too, smelled like they were baking bread...ugh! So far, I have not hit the normal crippling "carb flu" I get when I try to start low-carb.

My biggest question was about whether CT should occur simultaneously or if I should wait a few weeks as Melinda suggested. I tried face dunks when I first read the CT posts, but I got horrible brain freeze. I am currently not doing CT. I do wonder, though, is there a point at which I will be able to tell I am ready for CT? Like when the brain freeze goes away, or when I am LS?

### nonchalant — 2012-04-07T00:04:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/post-12286>

I'd try it periodically, and if the brain freeze goes away, then proceed cautiously and slowly.

It takes a while to get rid of excess Omega 6 oils in the tissues.

### Melinda — 2012-04-07T00:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/post-12291>

Hi SB, (if I may use initials, using your name might trigger an insulin response, ha, ha). When I started low/very low carb, I made sure I had plenty of protein and fat at each meal. I don't remember snacking much but if I couldn't resist now, I'd have a couple of TBP of coconut Manna by Nutria. To this day (4.5 yrs later) I don't go into grocery stores unless I'm well fed. Most non-paleo food is not tempting anymore but after a lifetime of hunger, I'm not willing to let that demon out of the bag again. I'd just do light CT for a while like ice (not gel packs) packs on your big parts while you watch tv. And like Nonchalant says, try the face once a week to check for progress. Good luck, may the low carb flu pass you by.

### Sugarbiscuit — 2012-04-07T03:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-central-washington.452/post-12342>

Holy cow, I never thought of that. My whole life people have been saying "Kandy" to me all day long. No wonder I'm so fat. Perpetual cascade of insulin...

(just kidding, I know where the perpetual cascade of insulin has really been coming from: SAD with a southern exaggeration)

Thanks for the input everyone!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Question to Dr. Kruse ...or anyone! HOW TO GET SOME OF THE FAT BACK???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-to-dr-kruse-or-anyone-how-to-get-some-of-the-fat-back.453/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nyika — 2012-03-27T02:02:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-to-dr-kruse-or-anyone-how-to-get-some-of-the-fat-back.453/post-6681>

So, after losing 5 kg (11lbs on hhcg and thinking that I am at my optimal weight) I went fully Paleo and lost another 2kg/4.4lbs without wanting to.

I now find that I have too little fat around my used to be curves.

Not much cushioning under my skin. Bruising all the time

I would like to look a little more... youthful or healthy, not too skinny and not too surfboard like (being a girl, I want to look like a girl, I don't want the Madonna look ).

What could I eat to actually put a little more weight on, just enough to get my girly curves back? Or have a little more cushioning under my skin?

Who would have thought that this Paleo/Leptin reset works THIS well??

I find that I can't eat a heap any more.

2 meals are usually enough, although I make sure that I eat 3 meals with the kids.

I try to eat sweet potato and other high carb veggies with each meal, but there is only so much I can stuff into my stomach.

I seriously NEED to get some more body fat back to fill my skin.

WHAT TO EAT? When? How much?

HELP!

### Guest — 2012-03-27T14:35:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-to-dr-kruse-or-anyone-how-to-get-some-of-the-fat-back.453/post-6922>

Question to Dr. Kruse provide us specific information so be conscious and get well demand as well as

growth anyone having some fat as well as physique and health.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-27T15:19:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-to-dr-kruse-or-anyone-how-to-get-some-of-the-fat-back.453/post-6934>

want to get fat.....eat safe starches at night or in the winter like most humans do.

### nyika — 2012-03-28T05:50:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-to-dr-kruse-or-anyone-how-to-get-some-of-the-fat-back.453/post-7580>

OK, thanks. Will try.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-11T03:22:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-to-dr-kruse-or-anyone-how-to-get-some-of-the-fat-back.453/post-13548>

Well having no curves or 'cushion' shouldn't mean you bruise easily...what is that? Check your labs instead. You might gain some fat from 'safe starches at night and in winter like the rest of humans', but will that even protect you from bruising? Check that one out before possibly ruining your hormones. What age are you? If past menopause, maybe check your hormone levels...etc.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-11T03:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-to-dr-kruse-or-anyone-how-to-get-some-of-the-fat-back.453/post-13549>

> **nyika;6717 wrote:** So, after losing 5 kg (11lbs on hhcg and thinking that I am at my optimal weight) I went fully Paleo and lost another 2kg/4.4lbs without wanting to.
>
> I now find that I have too little fat around my used to be curves.
>
> Not much cushioning under my skin. Bruising all the time
>
> I would like to look a little more... youthful or healthy, not too skinny and not too surfboard like (being a girl, I want to look like a girl, I don't want the Madonna look ).
>
> What could I eat to actually put a little more weight on, just enough to get my girly curves back? Or have a little more cushioning under my skin?
>
> Who would have thought that this Paleo/Leptin reset works THIS well??
>
>
>
> I find that I can't eat a heap any more.
>
> 2 meals are usually enough, although I make sure that I eat 3 meals with the kids.
>
> I try to eat sweet potato and other high carb veggies with each meal, but there is only so much I can stuff into my stomach.
>
>
>
> I seriously NEED to get some more body fat back to fill my skin.
>
> WHAT TO EAT? When? How much?
>
> HELP!

Dont eat a thing......use your ipod/ipad at night.....use your LED at night to screw your sleep up. this is how I gained 15 of my 25 pounds I needed to gain for my epic bio hack

### Honey Crisp — 2012-04-11T13:51:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-to-dr-kruse-or-anyone-how-to-get-some-of-the-fat-back.453/post-13685>

I lost a lot weight on HCG protocol and kept losing after finishing three rounds. If there is such thing as orthorexia. I had it. I was being asked if I was sick or not feeling well. It wasn't until my teenage daughter pointed out that I looked like I was "carrying a load" and my husband said my face looked gaunt, I realized I lost to much weight. I gained 7 pounds by eating more protein, cutting back on cardio and strength training. (I think my eating included a few trips to DQ, not recommended.) The 7 pounds was enough to fill out both sets of cheeks.

# Thread: Things to brush up on,  and perhaps come prepared with,  when trying a new doc
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-to-brush-up-on-and-perhaps-come-prepared-with-when-trying-a-new-doc.454/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-27T02:40:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-to-brush-up-on-and-perhaps-come-prepared-with-when-trying-a-new-doc.454/post-6705>

Ok,

So I really want to get the labs done, and so I've made an appointment with a new doc. I'm extremely nervous. I hate going into all of this. I'm a writer not a talker (classic introvert). What information/printouts etc would you think to be helpful when talking to a doc?

I've got the great list of Labs to request, but I'm pretty sure I can't just sit down and go "would you please just run these labs and not stress me out with your CW? Thanks"

Docs on here? what would you respond to? Are docs all worn out from patients bringing them websites?

### PaleoSue — 2012-03-27T03:41:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-to-brush-up-on-and-perhaps-come-prepared-with-when-trying-a-new-doc.454/post-6731>

I am pretty jaded when it comes to a regular doc. I personally would try to find a paleo doc or a D.O. who at least has heard of paleo. But I even had a D.O. who is supposed to be into alternative types of medicine tell me to stop eating fat because I'm overweight. \*sigh\*

On the plus side, you are able to order some lab tests for yourself. The downside is insurance won't pay for them.

### nonchalant — 2012-03-28T00:17:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-to-brush-up-on-and-perhaps-come-prepared-with-when-trying-a-new-doc.454/post-7394>

You could make a notation about why you want some of the tests..

Also, if you have copies of previous tests results that might be helpful.

The doctor has to be able to justify the tests for your insurance company. Also, he/she will want to run some basic stuff, just because you're new. You might have to wait for the second visit.

Jimmy Moore and Robb Wolfe have lists of doctors who understand the real science.

# Thread: Neoprene-Do you use it?  Feet in or out?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vwaggs — 2012-03-27T03:59:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-6742>

I used to keep my feet out of the bath but it was a pain so I bought the neoprene 5mm socks and it helps so much keeping my feet warm. However, I seem to be having more shivering and want to get out faster. I do have more detox symptoms now though too (4 weeks in) so that could also be part of it. I know this isn't a linear process but I thought the cold would be easier to take by now. I had ONE great bath that didn't make me shiver and my furnace kicked on right after and I was warm in no time. What happened?? It thought I was over the difficult part. I read Dr. K say on his blog to not use modern items (neoprene) to help cope. Use only what Mother Nature gave us. My feet hurt so bad in the cold though. Do I just leave them out of the water or tough it out? Seems like the cold would be harder to take with feet in. Who puts feet in? Arms? I guess I was under the impression to keep the extremeties out. What do you all do?

### DarleenMB — 2012-03-27T05:21:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-6782>

I'm not at the bath stage yet but I WILL be leaving my feet and hands OUT of the water. When my feet get chilled I cannot get warm. I say do what is comfortable for you at this point. If it's too much to keep your feet in the water, take 'em out.

### Vicster — 2012-03-27T05:25:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-6785>

I use the neoprene booties, water past my breasts, hands out. I have bouts of easy days and hard days in the last month, sometimes I shiver other times not. Detox is a funny thing. Dr. K says that even he still shivers form time to time. Be sure to eat a high fat/protein meal prior to CT and drink a couple of big glasses of ice water.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-27T05:47:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-6795>

FYI. Dr. Kruse's take on Neoprene booties:

Kami Says:

March 23rd, 2012 at 6:49 pm

HI Dr. K I have been doing the CT for 34 days on only missed 3 days. The first 3 weeks went well and I was wearing socks in the water. I was seeing improvements in mood and sleep. In the 4th week I added neoprene booties and now I am shivering in the water and not craving the water anymore. Could the booties have something to do with it?

Jack Says:

March 24th, 2012 at 8:43 am

@Kami……stop using modern things to offset the program…..use what mother nature gave you.

PS-I just keep my feet out for now because of Reyanuds. Sometimes my toes turn white EVEN outside the water.

### vwaggs — 2012-03-27T15:53:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-6959>

Patty Cakes that was the exact post I was referring to! My feet would get numb for hours even out of the tub with slipper socks on. The Neoprene helped this issue a lot.

Guess I will try putting in my feet tonight to see what happens. They hurt just thinking about it LOL! I wonder how many others put in their feet?? Did you do it right away or work up to it? Am I missing out by not putting in my feet or will I adapt just fine keeping them out?

### agatha — 2012-03-27T16:50:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-6992>

I use neoprene gloves in the bath - they really help. But I do cold water dunking of my hands at other times to help the adaptation. I will try without the gloves soon. I don't have socks but I take my feet out of the water after i first get in and then gradually lower them in towards the end of the time - by this time it feels quite pleasant to do this.

### sk1nny_f4t — 2012-03-27T17:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7018>

> **vwaggs;6996 wrote:** I wonder how many others put in their feet?? Did you do it right away or work up to it? Am I missing out by not putting in my feet or will I adapt just fine keeping them out?

My experience has been opposite of yours, I just dove right in, 3 soaks down wearing only a knit hat in a 50degree trash can filled with water and ice. I crouch so that the water is just below my chest and each of the times I have done 30 min soaks it takes at least an hour, if not more, for my feet to come back to normal. If you have ever been reading on the toilet for so long that your feet fall asleep, that's how mine feel so I am ordering a pair of the booties today.

### freshveggies — 2012-03-27T17:44:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7035>

HI Vwaggs, I forgot to reply back to you about the neoprene on the blog. I have used very light weight wool socks in the tub, but am considering not using anything based on what Dr. K said. My 06/03 ratio is good so I shouldn't have a problem with the water on my feet. I think it will help me adapt better if I dont' cover my feet.

### freshveggies — 2012-03-27T17:48:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7037>

I get in the tub then turn the cold water on and let it fill. Since I quite the neoprene, I don't shiver in the water like I was after I started the neoprene. I just thought it was a short cut and would work. No cold feet---how nice. It back fired.

### ealachan — 2012-03-27T18:14:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7061>

This is not particularly helpful to the point of the thread at all, I know, but I just have to say that the mental images I conjure up of y'all trying to keep hands and feet out of the bath while soaking amuse me to no end. I wish I was a better artist - this begs for cartooning...

### Susan M. — 2012-03-27T18:52:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7102>

> **Vicster;6821 wrote:** I use the neoprene booties, water past my breasts, hands out. I have bouts of easy days and hard days in the last month, sometimes I shiver other times not. Detox is a funny thing. Dr. K says that even he still shivers form time to time. Be sure to eat a high fat/protein meal prior to CT and drink a couple of big glasses of ice water.

Is shivering while you are IN the water a sign of detoxing? I notice that only the parts of me that are in the water are shivering. My legs, while in the water, shiver to the point of almost being painful.

As for the feet, I used the neoprene booties at first to keep the spray from the faucet off of my feet while the tub was filling up. Now, I can submerge my feet with the booties on. I take them off after the first ten minutes or so, but I wind up putting them back on because they still feel better on in the water. I will be glad when I don't need them any more.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-27T19:05:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7125>

I've been doing cold baths for 3 weeks now and up until a couple of days ago, was using neoprene booties. After reading that exchange on the blog, I switched to wool socks. My toes get quite cold and my feet feel "tingly" afterwards, but in an odd way, that tingly sensation feels good. I was afraid I'd get that awful pins & needles feeling you get when your feet are really cold, but not at all.

I haven't noticed a difference in how much I shiver while in the tub. It seems that if I put my upper arms in the water, I shiver off-and-on throughout, but if I keep them out, I don't shiver until the last few minutes. I'm working up to more arm immersion, but slowly. Much as I usually like to rush through things, I've decided on this one to listen to my body and go at a reasonable pace.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-03-27T19:08:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7131>

I put my feet in. The first week of baths it made my feet really ache. No problems with it now though. In fact, yesterday my husband and I spent almost an hour in our in-ground MICHIGAN pool. Pool thermometer said it was 56 degrees. The outside temp was 42 degrees. If any of the neighbors saw us, they probably think we've gone mad!

### Vicster — 2012-03-27T21:58:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7312>

> **Susan M.;7139 wrote:** Is shivering while you are IN the water a sign of detoxing? I notice that only the parts of me that are in the water are shivering. My legs, while in the water, shiver to the point of almost being painful.
>
>
>
> As for the feet, I used the neoprene booties at first to keep the spray from the faucet off of my feet while the tub was filling up. Now, I can submerge my feet with the booties on. I take them off after the first ten minutes or so, but I wind up putting them back on because they still feel better on in the water. I will be glad when I don't need them any more.

I don't shiver while in the water. It takes about 4 minutes to fill the tub while I sit in it and that to me is the hardest part. After the tub is full I am comfortable. It's when I get out and towel off that I sometimes shiver, sometimes not. Since I am feeling comfortable in the water I will try it without the booties and see how my feet feel. Before I used them I could hardly walk after CT, but that's been a few weeks ago so it might be better now.

### Ane — 2012-03-27T23:19:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7368>

Wow...I never thought about booties. I just get in and my feet go numb within a couple of minutes.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-27T23:30:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7376>

I've been using wool socks. Works for me. No issues during or after the bath, so I'm gonna stick with this, until I build up the courage to go barefoot in the tub. For now, having compression shorts and top, with the socks and a hat help me psychologically, even though I know I don't \*need\* them. I'm going to work towards weaning myself of them and going nekkid.

### differentstory — 2012-03-28T18:48:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7766>

> **Vicster;7350 wrote:** I don't shiver while in the water. It takes about 4 minutes to fill the tub while I sit in it and that to me is the hardest part. After the tub is full I am comfortable. It's when I get out and towel off that I sometimes shiver, sometimes not. Since I am feeling comfortable in the water I will try it without the booties and see how my feet feel. Before I used them I could hardly walk after CT, but that's been a few weeks ago so it might be better now.

I had this problem too, Vicster. I found that the noise of the water pouring in and the cold water splashes aggrevated me. So now, instead, last night I filled the tub up and then got in when it was quiet. A MILLION TIMES BETTER! It was definitely hard to sit in the tub at first but I just sat down, counted to 43 seconds and I was fine. I will be doing that now on.

Also, I use the booties as well. Maybe one day I won't, but for now this is hard enough. I think we should take it slow and I personally think shivering and booties have nothing to do with each other. I have found that eating and shivering do though. If I haven't eaten as much I find my body seems to lack the energy to keep me warm and I start to shiver. Just my theory (and I have a lot of those! HA!).

My feet get so cold that I keep glancing at them thinking they must be blue. That was why I started the booties. And I remember thinking, "if my feet didn't hurt so damned much, this wouldn't even be hard!!" So that's why I got the booties.

### youthfuliz — 2012-03-28T18:55:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7770>

I've got neoprene booties after reading about them here.  I use them in cold bath/shower and keep my feet in water. I take them off for the last 10 minutes though since by then I feel like I want my feet to be at the same temperature as the rest of my body.  That's also why I tend to wash my hair and let cold water run down the back of my head toward the end of the cold shower.

### JC2K — 2012-03-28T19:02:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7773>

Personally, I'm planning on using the booties/gloves until I cold adapt more, then take them off. So it's a short term thing rather than a permanent thing.

### youthfuliz — 2012-03-28T19:03:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7774>

And for those of you who have cold feet for hours after the cold bath/shower, I found a way to fix it: use your feet for 10 minutes after the shower/bath, do a little running/walking/jumping/leg lifts with ankle weights, etc, get blood running in your legs/feet, they will become warm within minutes.  It works for me!

### JC2K — 2012-03-28T19:12:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7779>

> **Susan M.;7139 wrote:** Is shivering while you are IN the water a sign of detoxing?

Yes and no. The shivering is not a symptom of detoxing. The shivering is your body trying to warm itself up, causing your body to draw on those fat cells for energy (at some point, depending on your food).The detoxing comes from the emptying of the fat cells for energy. But I have no idea how much at one time. It's not like you use a pound of fat in one session. So the toxins come out because of shivering, you don't shiver because of toxins. Clearer?

### Susan M. — 2012-03-28T19:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7801>

> **JC2K;7821 wrote:** Yes and no. The shivering is not a symptom of detoxing. The shivering is your body trying to warm itself up, causing your body to draw on those fat cells for energy (at some point, depending on your food).The detoxing comes from the emptying of the fat cells for energy. But I have no idea how much at one time. It's not like you use a pound of fat in one session. So the toxins come out because of shivering, you don't shiver because of toxins. Clearer?

Thank you JC2K for the explanation. I was thinking about it last night, and I guess I should have said that I have major goosebump on my legs while I am in the water. They really look unnatural. I don't have any goosebumps on my top half while I am in the water, and most of my noticeable shivering is done after I have been out of the tub for about five to ten minutes and continues sporadically for about 30 minutes.

Thanks again for the response. A little more clear, yes. It takes me a long time to figure out things. I took a statin drug for only about a year, but it really messed up my ability to focus, and I am still recovering I suspect. I know that is a poor excuse, but it is what it is, but eventually I get there. I haven't noticed much in the way of detoxing, I guess. Just starting to get some loose squishy fat on my stomach. I notice that the next day, if I haven't done my soak yet, that it has firmed up just a bit. I really don't know what that is all about. I guess I should hope for some more out-of-the tub shivering?

This is all so facinating!

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-03-28T19:57:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7803>

I use the booties I find if I don't my feet cramp badly and I can't stay in the water. even with the booties I can get cramping but my water temp is about 47F

### Jim — 2012-03-28T20:42:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-7846>

No booties for me. I get the water running then add some Epsom Salts. I step in barefooted and help the salts dissolve with my feet. Then I sit down while the tub "fills". The water only comes up to about my waist so my upper body is out of the water. I have ice packs on my thighs, the tops of which are exposed, and on my tummy. I move them around a bit to make sure I'm not getting frostbite. When I take my cold shower I also start with my legs and move up body. Of course with the shower I am getting everything wet. The back goes last but by then I am fairly acclimated. My feet are now fine using this process. When I first started they would be a bit stiff. Give it time and things will get better.

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-13T06:46:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neoprene-do-you-use-it-feet-in-or-out.456/post-14599>

I haven't used the neoprene booties for two weeks now. I find it's sufficient to keep my feet out from time to time. I alternate between sitting up with water up to my stomach, feet in, and full body immersion, up to my neck, and keep my feet out. I'll keep the booties to use in the future when I walk in a lake - in case the sand is not smooth or there're shells...

# Thread: I have an important question for Dr. Kruse, a friend said he is a good person to ask
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-an-important-question-for-dr-kruse-a-friend-said-he-is-a-good-person-to-ask.457/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ertarox — 2012-03-27T04:19:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-an-important-question-for-dr-kruse-a-friend-said-he-is-a-good-person-to-ask.457/post-6752>

Hi,

I have studied diet, nutrition, all sorts of health modalities, and on a mad pursuit to figure out what was wrong with me. I saw a lot of people reverse cancer and all sorts of problems using nutrition, and I know that diet and exercise are fundamental.

I suspected I had insulin-resistance, imbalanced hormones and systemic yeast/candida. I knew I had that, actually, and had some testing here and there.....but I had a harder then hell time following any one protocol for long and even when I did, results were marginal, nothing like others experience. I only felt better if I quit eating altogether, which seemed to balance me a bit. But I have abused that far too much, and of course it is devastating on the body.

I had a work injury, went through that nightmare for 9 months (about to finally have my knee scoped for it) and then got fired. Shortly thereafter had falling outs with my brother and father and of course most friends fell away....it was all a blessing as it simplified life and the real people that matter will value you unconditionally.

I basically made a very severe decision to use every penny of credit available to me to charge all the medical tests I could never ever afford, determined to figure this out (and I am VERY good at sourcing underlying causes of health issues for others at this point) or to finally just end my life. It's been decades of this.

I hired a top ND at The Tahoma Clinic, world pioneers in bio-identical hormones and practitioners of orthomolecular medicine, and I hired a psychic that a no-bullshit friend highly recommended. And then a really good psychologist.

From my organic acids test it was determined that my mitochondria are not converting ATP, I have severe neurotransmitter deficiency, it was one of the worst she has seen in her career. Major malabsorption issues to where she would put me on nutritional I.V., but she is in Washington, I am in IL, and that is likely a byproduct of my self-abusive coping mechanisms too.

While all the tests told me something, my hormone test was awful. My HGH was incredibly high (and I never slept so that made so sense). It was so high I had to test for a pituitary tumor, but had none. My progesterone and estrogen were more than just "low", and so I started on those and in a couple months I started sleeping for the first time in over 20 years. I never slept, which is just awful. DHEA was horribly low, adrenals low....

I couldn't believe I didn't have a pituitary tumor. That would explain so much. I started researching what would ruin my pituitary and nuerotransmitters and adrenals in such a way, and kept coming up on TRAUMA. Obviously, stress kills....but I started reading about PTSD, something I knew little about but knew of......and knew without a doubt that THIS WAS ME. But for as far back as I can remember. And it wreaks havoc on the H-P-A axis (and a few other key things).

I paid to see a very qualified psychologist with experience in trauma, and did all the tests, the oral history. I definitely have severe PTSD.  There was extreme emotional abuse from ages 11 - adulthood, but even then....why did I have these vision problems, spatial issues, balance problems, extreme moodswings, this....FEELING...in my head. My ND said she wasn't convinced there wasn't a brain issue either. I have a vision problem not stemming from my eyes AT ALL. But the MRI I did was "fine" but for a small lesion in the brainstem. The EEG the psychologist did was all over the place, so non-integrated it would be similar to that found in something more than PTSD.

And why did I have PTSD feelings and symptoms dated PRIOR to eleven years old? Things still were't checking out, but seeing as how I stumped my ND too, I didn't feel as bad. It's all just too strange. There's more symptoms too, but to tie this up, the only possible conclusion considering all of this, my kind of glum side even as a kid (my mom says), my entire life, my twin brother's legal blindness and all the tubes he had in his ears, things my dad said and how explosive he was, my mother's hollowness, as if traumatized herself....is that I had a brain injury. Very very early on. And from being shaken. Which is just devastating.

While I consciously forgot, my body remembered. PTSD. And brain injury. I have some kind of brain damage. And I believe the issue in my brainstem is neurovascular. The MRI I have is not the right kind of imaging for microtears or for my brainstem issue. They assumed it was just Capillary telangiectasias, which is generally benign. This is all but.

My question is not seeking medical advice or anything. I am able to schedule with a neurologist in about a month, I PRAY. It has to be done in a certain way, which really really sucks but that's the insurance world. I may not get to at all and cannot even think of that possibility. But my question is, is Capillary telangiectasias ever this disruptive? It's a 3mm lesion in the pons area, presumably that. Because you are a neurosurgeon, what else could I be researching in the meantime? I know it can be scar tissue, from a ligamental tear. It's right by the craniocervical junction and I FEEL IT. I pinch it and it offers some relief. I KNOW there is a injury there.

This part fascinates me, too:

Last September, I discovered that the xanax I was taking for sleep for the past 8 months (I finally resorted to scripts after averaging 4 hours sleep every 3 or 4 days, total), that I switched to due to the work injury, had accidentally become an addiction. The symptoms were disrupting my work injury symptoms, which is bad. I apparently was going through interdose withdrawal for months, not knowing it. I knew the risks but just cold-turkeyed it. I learned it was xanax addiction after accidentally withdrawing for about 48 hours once or twice before, then taking it. I could not believe alllll of that was xanax, I thought I was dying and you can die. I had paid attention, and I figured out fast that I needed to wear a neckbrace interestingly enough. Then the w/d went okay (as long as I stayed upright).

Why this is INTERESTING is that(I since learned that) in xanax withdrawal, it blows out (or something) all your calcium chloride. I am not a doctor, but I observed, then learned then researched it, that any tiny injury will be blown up to grotesque proportions when going under xanax w/d for this reason. My meniscus tear was sensitive, but I needed a brace and was limping profusely at night, versus could walk damn near fine during the day, when not going through withdrawal. This is key to me because the TWO sources of major dysfunction were my knee, where there was a known meniscus tear, and my neck. I felt it at the top of my neck (brainstem) and I could not hold my head up. I took a cab to the E.R where a friend is a doctor just to get a neck brace. I wore it for a week. It disabled my knee and my neck, grossly blowing the problem out of proportion. But my brainstem has been a source of frustration my whole life. So I am oddly grateful for the experience because I KNOW there's an issue. There IS an injury. And my vestibular-ocular (sp?) pathway is affected as

Can a ligament tear or scar tissue cause a neurovascular problem, or can Capillary telangiectasias be that disruptive?

And is the leptin diet key for neurotransmitters and/or pituitary health? I know I'll be doing orthomolecular doses of supplements, I have a lot right here. Linus Pauling style. And Dr. Perlmutter is applying nutritional science to neurogenesis and having success. Neuroplasticity is available to us but PTSD is a huge interference. Still, I try, and I educate myself as best I can. I plan to pursue neurofeedback for the PTSD as soon as I can rule out a neurovascular issue or have it corrected.

I've been trying to treat all this with wheatgrass and diet! Kinda funny to me, overall. LOL!  Gotta laugh at yourself.

Thanks so much. I know it's a tacky large forum post and many show up with huge life sagas and little interest in doing the research or work themselves. I tried to give a background and not a sob story. I hope I stuck to relevant issues only. I'm usually the guy coaching people in those situations. I just figured this out literally over the holidays, with the PTSD confirmation in Jan. Then I ran out of money and the depression set in. I've loved studying neuroscience the best I can, but it's a lot to digest. So it never hurts to ask!

### hazyjane — 2012-03-27T06:11:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-an-important-question-for-dr-kruse-a-friend-said-he-is-a-good-person-to-ask.457/post-6804>

Hi, Ertarox!

I'm not Dr. Kruse, but I have a suggestion for you:

I used to be a neurofeedback practitioner and I think you could benefit from Brain State Conditioning (a particular form of neurofeedback). It's very effective at reversing PTSD and opening up new neural networks to operate from. You can literally rewire your brain. As long as you are supporting your brain through neurotransmitter support and diet, I think your brain has a shot at making some good progress with Brain State.

I would suggest that if you do it, get the practitioner to consult with Lee Gerdes, the founder, on your case. It will be extra money, but it'll be money well spent, since Lee knows how to correct brain imbalances like nobody else.

[http://brainstatetech.com/](http://http://brainstatetech.com/)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-27T06:15:00+0300
